steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Andromeda — home of the Andromeda Galaxy, the most distant object visible to the naked eye.

steps.title: Follow the W to the Great Square
steps.caption: The third zag of Cassiopeia's W points to Andromeda's brightest star, Alpheratz, at one corner of the Great Square of Pegasus.

steps.title: Three stars in a chain
steps.caption: Andromeda's backbone is a curving chain of three bright stars: Alpheratz, Mirach, and Almach. They stretch away from the Great Square in a gentle arc.

steps.title: The myth of Andromeda
steps.caption: Andromeda is at the center of a six-constellation mythological group. Her father, King Cepheus, offers her as a sacrifice to the sea monster Cetus, hoping to appease a god offended by her mother Cassiopeia's vanity. She's rescued in the nick of time by Perseus, who rides to her on the winged horse Pegasus.

steps.title: The Andromeda Galaxy
steps.caption: The Andromeda Galaxy is the farthest object most people will ever see with unaided eyes—2.5 million light-years. Look for a fuzzy oval near Mirach—under dark skies it appears several times wider than the full Moon.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Antlia — the Air Pump, one of the faintest constellations in the sky, invented by Lacaille in the 1750s and lying between Hydra and Vela in the southern spring sky.

steps.title: Between Hydra and Vela
steps.caption: From Alphard — the solitary orange star that marks Hydra's heart — drop south toward the bright stars of Vela. Antlia sits in that empty-looking gap. It is best seen from February to April and is invisible north of about 45°N.

steps.title: Lacaille's air pump
steps.caption: Antlia honors the vacuum pump, a cutting-edge scientific instrument of the Age of Enlightenment. The region contains NGC 2997, a face-on spiral galaxy about 40 million light-years away visible in a small telescope from dark southern sites.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Apus — the Bird of Paradise, a small faint constellation near the south celestial pole.

steps.title: South of the Southern Triangle
steps.caption: Triangulum Australe, the compact bright southern triangle, is the key landmark. Apus lies in the sparse region between it and the south celestial pole.

steps.title: Bird of Paradise
steps.caption: Apus was named by Dutch navigators Keyser and de Houtman in the late 16th century, after the exotic Birds of Paradise encountered on their East Indies voyages — birds so beautiful they were believed to spend their entire lives in flight, never touching the ground.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Aquarius — the Water Bearer, a sprawling zodiacal constellation. It has no bright stars, so dark skies help considerably.

steps.title: Find the Water Jar
steps.caption: The Y-shaped Water Jar asterism is the key landmark. Fomalhaut — the lone bright star to the south in Piscis Austrinus — is a useful anchor: the Water Jar floats above it. Sadalsud, the brightest star in Aquarius, sits just to the west of the jar.

steps.title: Two remarkable nebulae
steps.caption: The Saturn Nebula and the Helix Nebula both lie within Aquarius. The Helix is the closest planetary nebula to Earth at about 650 light-years — in a telescope it appears as a ghostly ring, larger than a full moon. The Saturn Nebula earns its name from the lobes flanking its disc, visible in larger telescopes.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Aquila — the Eagle, whose heart is Altair, the southernmost star of the Summer Triangle.

steps.title: Straddling the Milky Way
steps.caption: Aquila straddles the Milky Way right on the celestial equator, making it visible from every inhabited place on Earth. Altair is the brightest star in the constellation and one of the closest naked-eye stars to us, just 17 light-years away.

steps.title: The eagle's head
steps.caption: Altair is flanked closely by Tarazed to the north and Alshain to the south — three stars nearly in a line that form the eagle's head. Tarazed has a warm orange tint that sets it apart from the other two.

steps.title: Summer Triangle
steps.caption: Altair forms the Summer Triangle with Vega in Lyra and Deneb in Cygnus. Together they span a wide swath of the Milky Way and form the most prominent star pattern in the evening sky during northern summer and southern winter.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Ara — the Altar, a compact southern constellation just south of Scorpius's stinger.

steps.title: Below the scorpion's tail
steps.caption: Find Shaula, the brightest star at the tip of the scorpion's stinger, then look south — the compact outline of the Altar sits just beyond. The Milky Way runs richly through this region.

steps.title: The ancient altar
steps.caption: Ara is one of the 48 original Ptolemaic constellations — the altar upon which the Olympian gods swore their oaths before the Titanomachy, the great war against the Titans. NGC 6397 is one of the nearest globular clusters to Earth at about 7,800 light-years — visible as a fuzzy star in binoculars.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Aries — the Ram, a small but historically important zodiacal constellation that was once the location of the vernal equinox.

steps.title: West of the Pleiades
steps.caption: The Pleiades cluster in Taurus is the easiest landmark — Aries lies just to the west of it, a small curved arc of stars in an otherwise sparse region.

steps.title: Hamal, Sheratan, and Mesarthim
steps.caption: Hamal is the brightest star, an orange giant. Sheratan forms an obvious pair with it. Mesarthim, the faintest of the three, is historically notable — it was one of the first double stars ever observed through a telescope.

steps.title: The First Point of Aries
steps.caption: Though small and modest, Aries was the most important constellation in ancient astronomy: the vernal equinox — where the Sun crosses the celestial equator each March — was once located here. Due to precession it has since drifted into Pisces, but the term “First Point of Aries” endures as the name for the vernal equinox direction.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Auriga — the Charioteer, anchored by Capella, one of the brightest stars in the sky and easy to spot by its warm golden colour.

steps.title: Find Capella
steps.caption: If you can see Orion, Capella is the bright golden star on the far side of the belt from Sirius. Look for a prominent pentagon of stars with Capella at the top — that is Auriga.

steps.title: Capella and the Kids
steps.caption: Capella is actually two golden giant stars — both remarkably similar to the Sun in colour, but far more luminous. Three faint stars called the Kids cluster nearby. One of them, Epsilon Aurigae, is a remarkable eclipsing system: a supergiant eclipsed every 27 years by a vast disc of unknown material.

steps.title: Three Messier clusters
steps.caption: The Milky Way passes through Auriga, bringing three fine open clusters: M36, M37, and M38. All three are visible as hazy patches in binoculars and resolve into beautiful star fields in a small telescope.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let’s find Boötes — the Herdsman, home to Arcturus, the brightest star north of the celestial equator and an unmistakably orange beacon in the sky.

steps.title: Arc to Arcturus
steps.caption: The classic star-hop: follow the curved arc of the Big Dipper’s handle outward and you land on Arcturus. “Arc to Arcturus” is one of the most reliable mnemonics in astronomy.

steps.title: Arcturus
steps.caption: Arcturus is the fourth-brightest star in the night sky — its orange colour unmistakable. It moves through space at an unusually high velocity relative to the Sun, a member of the older thick disk of the Milky Way with a very different orbital history from most nearby stars.

steps.title: The kite
steps.caption: Boötes forms a kite shape with Arcturus at its southern tip. Izar, on the side of the kite, is a stunning orange-and-blue double star in a small telescope — nicknamed Pulcherrima, “most beautiful,” by astronomers.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let’s find Caelum — the Chisel, one of the smallest and faintest constellations, invented by Lacaille and tucked between Eridanus, Columba, and Horologium in the southern sky.

steps.title: Between Eridanus and Columba
steps.caption: Caelum sits in a sparse corner of the southern sky, nestled beside the winding river Eridanus. Rigel in Orion is the brightest nearby star — look south from there into the empty-looking region between Eridanus and Columba.

steps.title: Lacaille’s engraving tool
steps.caption: Caelum honors the burin — the engraving tool used by printmakers and sculptors. One of 14 instrument constellations Lacaille introduced after his 1751–52 Cape of Good Hope survey, it is among the most challenging to identify, with no bright stars and no deep-sky objects of note. A completionist’s target.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Camelopardalis — the Giraffe, a very large but very faint circumpolar constellation filling the gap between Polaris and Auriga.

steps.title: Sprawling but dim
steps.caption: The left arm of Cassiopeia's W points to the dark void between Polaris and Capella where the Giraffe lives. It's the third largest constellation by area, but easy to miss because its main stars are so faint.

steps.title: NGC 2403
steps.caption: Invented by Petrus Plancius in 1613, it represents the giraffe that carried Rebekah to Isaac. Its most rewarding target is NGC 2403 — one of the nearest spiral galaxies beyond our Local Group and a fine binocular object from dark skies.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Cancer — the Crab, the faintest of the zodiacal constellations, but home to the beautiful Beehive, a naked-eye star cluster known since antiquity.

steps.title: Look between Gemini and Leo
steps.caption: Cancer sits in the sparse gap between Pollux and Castor in Gemini and bright Regulus in Leo. Cancer's inconspicuous stars make it an ideal dark-sky test: if you can see them with the naked eye, conditions are excellent.

steps.title: Spot the Beehive Cluster
steps.caption: The Beehive is a fuzzy patch at the center of Cancer, visible as a smear of light under dark skies and resolving in binoculars into dozens of stars. It is one of the nearest open clusters at about 610 light-years.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Canes Venatici — the Hunting Dogs, a small constellation below the handle of the Big Dipper, anchored by the bright blue-white star Cor Caroli.

steps.title: Below the Big Dipper handle
steps.caption: Find Alkaid — the star at the tip of the Dipper's handle. Cor Caroli is the bright blue-white star sitting just below it.

steps.title: Cor Caroli
steps.caption: Cor Caroli (‘Charles’s Heart’) is a beautiful double star in a small telescope and the brightest of the two named stars in the constellation. Nearby shines Chara. Despite having only two named stars, the constellation is rich in background galaxies.

steps.title: Galaxy country
steps.caption: Canes Venatici sits away from the Milky Way’s dust, giving clear sightlines into the deep universe. M51, the Whirlpool Galaxy, is one of the most photogenic spirals in the sky. M3 is among the finest globular clusters — visible in binoculars as a fuzzy star.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Canis Major — the Great Dog, home of Sirius, the brightest star in the night sky.

steps.title: Find the Dog Star
steps.caption: Canis Major follows Orion across the sky. Sirius marks the dog’s shoulder. Follow the line of Orion's belt and you'll find it. The ancient Egyptians timed the annual Nile flood by the first dawn rising of Sirius and called it Sopdet.

steps.title: The Great Dog
steps.caption: Adhara, at the hindquarters, is intrinsically far more luminous than Sirius, but appears dimmer because it's nearly 30 times as far away.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Canis Minor — the Lesser Dog, a tiny constellation defined almost entirely by one brilliant star: Procyon, the eighth-brightest in the night sky.

steps.title: The Winter Triangle
steps.caption: Procyon forms the Winter Triangle with Sirius and Betelgeuse. If you can spot those two, Procyon completes the triangle.

steps.title: Two stars
steps.caption: Canis Minor has only two stars worth finding: Procyon and Gomeisa. The dog body is largely imaginary — there is little else to see. Procyon is one of the Sun's nearest neighbors and, like Sirius, has a white dwarf companion detectable only with large telescopes.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Capricornus — the Sea Goat, one of the oldest recognized constellations. Its stars are faint but form a distinctive arrowhead shape.

steps.title: Follow the Beak
steps.caption: Start at Altair in Aquila and follow the direction of the eagle's beak.

steps.title: Find Algedi and Dabih
steps.caption: The tip of the arrowhead nearest Aquila is marked by Algedi and Dabih. Look closely at Algedi — it's actually two unrelated stars that happen to line up, forming one of the easiest naked-eye doubles in the sky.

steps.title: The sea goat's outline
steps.caption: Deneb Algedi at the tail is the brightest star — an eclipsing binary that dips every 24.5 hours. Neptune was first spotted in Capricornus in 1846.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Carina — the Keel, the brightest part of the ancient ship Argo Navis. It contains Canopus, the second-brightest star in the entire sky.

steps.title: Find Canopus
steps.caption: Sirius is the brightest star in the sky. Canopus is the second. Look well south of Sirius for another blazing star.

steps.title: The Ship Argo
steps.caption: Carina was once part of Argo Navis — the great ship of Jason and the Argonauts, and the largest of Ptolemy's original 48 constellations. Lacaille split it into three parts in 1755: Carina (the keel), Vela (the sails), and Puppis (the stern).

steps.title: The False Cross
steps.caption: Two of Carina's bright stars — orange Avior and Aspidiske — join two stars in neighbouring Vela to form the False Cross, a diamond sometimes mistaken for the Southern Cross.

steps.title: Eta Carinae Nebula
steps.caption: The Eta Carinae Nebula is one of the largest star-forming regions in the Milky Way, visible to the naked eye as a bright patch. The unstable hypergiant Eta Carinae within it may one day become a supernova. Nearby is the Southern Pleiades, one of the finest open clusters in the sky.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Cassiopeia — the Queen, one of the most recognizable patterns in the sky. Five bright stars form an unmistakable W shape.

steps.title: Across from the Big Dipper
steps.caption: Cassiopeia and the Big Dipper sit on opposite sides of Polaris. Find the W on the far side of the pole from the Dipper's handle.

steps.title: The W
steps.caption: Schedar and Caph mark one end of the W, with Cih at the sharp central peak. The W — or M, depending on its orientation — is unlike any other pattern near the pole.

steps.title: The myth of Andromeda
steps.caption: Cassiopeia is part of the largest mythological group in the sky — six neighbouring constellations tell the story of Andromeda's rescue from a sea monster.

steps.title: The Queen's vanity
steps.caption: Cassiopeia boasted she was more beautiful than the sea nymphs, angering Poseidon, which eventually placed her daughter Andromeda in mortal peril. As punishment the Queen is forever bound to her throne, circling the pole and never setting (in northern latitudes).

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Centaurus — the Centaur, a large southern constellation anchored by two of the brightest stars in the sky.

steps.title: The Southern Pointers
steps.caption: Two brilliant stars close together — Rigil Kentaurus and Hadar — are the Centaur's front hooves. They're called the Southern Pointers because a line through them leads straight to the Southern Cross.

steps.title: Nearest star
steps.caption: Rigil Kentaurus, also popularly known as Alpha Centauri, is the nearest star system to the Sun and the third-brightest star in the night sky. Together with Hadar, the pair is unmistakable.

steps.title: The full centaur
steps.caption: Menkent marks the Centaur's head, far from the bright pointers at the hooves. Omega Centauri, the sky's brightest globular cluster, is visible as a fuzzy star near the boundary.

steps.title: A forgotten sky
steps.caption: Centaurus is one of the oldest constellations — the Babylonians mapped a centaur figure over 3,000 years ago, and the Greeks associated it with Chiron, the wise teacher of heroes. Precession has since carried it below the European horizon; stars the ancients knew by heart are now a purely southern sight.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Cepheus — the King, shaped like a child's drawing of a house. Look for it between Cassiopeia and Polaris.

steps.title: Between Cassiopeia and Polaris
steps.caption: Find Cassiopeia's W first. Cepheus is the blunt pentagon on the Polaris side of the W.

steps.title: The house shape
steps.caption: Alderamin marks one corner of the base. The triangular roof points toward Polaris. None of the stars are especially bright, but the house shape is easy to spot once you know where to look.

steps.title: The myth of Andromeda
steps.caption: Cepheus is part of the largest mythological group in the sky — six neighbouring constellations tell the story of Andromeda's rescue from a sea monster.

steps.title: The reluctant King
steps.caption: Cepheus is Cassiopeia's husband — the king who reluctantly agreed to sacrifice his daughter Andromeda to appease Poseidon's sea monster. The Garnet Star on the base of the house is one of the largest and reddest stars visible to the naked eye.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Cetus — the sea monster from the Andromeda myth, one of the largest constellations in the sky.

steps.title: Below the Great Square
steps.caption: Start at the Great Square of Pegasus and look below it. Diphda, the brightest star in Cetus, stands out in an otherwise sparse region.

steps.title: Diphda and Menkar
steps.caption: Diphda marks the tail and Menkar marks the jaw. A curving chain of faint stars connects them, recognizable from dark sites but hard to trace under bright skies.

steps.title: The myth of Andromeda
steps.caption: Cetus is the sea monster sent by Poseidon to devour Andromeda — part of the largest mythological group in the sky, spanning six neighbouring constellations.

steps.title: Mira the Wonderful
steps.caption: Mira is one of the sky's most famous variable stars, pulsating over about 332 days. At maximum it outshines every other star in Cetus; at minimum it vanishes entirely. Tau Ceti, just 12 light-years away, is one of the nearest Sun-like stars.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Chamaeleon — the Chameleon, a small faint constellation near the south celestial pole.

steps.title: Between two landmarks
steps.caption: Chamaeleon lies roughly halfway between the Large Magellanic Cloud and Rigil Kentaurus, in a sparse region with few bright stars.

steps.title: A loose kite of faint stars
steps.caption: Four faint stars form a loose kite shape. Dark skies are needed to trace the pattern — none of the stars stands out on its own.

steps.title: The Chameleon
steps.caption: Chamaeleon was introduced by the Dutch navigators Keyser and de Houtman around 1598. It hosts the Chamaeleon Cloud, one of the closest active star-forming regions to Earth.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Circinus — the Drawing Compass, one of the smallest constellations in the sky, tucked right next to the Southern Pointers.

steps.title: Next to the Southern Pointers
steps.caption: Circinus sits right beside the Southern Pointers (so named because they point at the Southern Cross). Find Rigil Kentaurus — the brighter of the two — and Alpha Circini is the next noticeable star beside it.

steps.title: The tiny compass
steps.caption: Three faint stars form the compass shape — so small the whole constellation fits in a single binocular field.

steps.title: The Circinus Galaxy
steps.caption: Circinus hides a remarkable object: the Circinus Galaxy, one of the nearest active galaxies at 13 million light-years. It's heavily obscured by the Milky Way's dust lanes and requires a telescope to spot.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Columba — the Dove, a small constellation south of Lepus the Hare, anchored by the blue-white star Phact.

steps.title: South of Orion and Lepus
steps.caption: Phact sits roughly halfway between Rigel and Canopus, two of the brightest stars in the sky. Look for it just past Lepus the Hare.

steps.title: Phact and Wazn
steps.caption: Blue-white Phact and orange Wazn mark the body of the Dove — a nice color contrast in binoculars.

steps.title: Noah's dove
steps.caption: Columba likely represents Noah's dove returning with an olive branch. The faint star Mu Columbae is a runaway — a blue star hurled from the Orion Nebula region at over 100 km/s by a supernova explosion millions of years ago, now racing away through this quiet corner of sky.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Coma Berenices — Berenice's Hair, a large sparse constellation whose stars form a glittering open cluster visible to the naked eye from dark sites.

steps.title: Between Leo and Boötes
steps.caption: Coma Berenices lies between Leo and Boötes. Look past Denebola, Leo's tail, for a large, loose, fuzzy glow. That's the Coma Star Cluster, 40 stars at 290 light-years, one of the sky's finest binocular clusters.

steps.title: Galaxy country
steps.caption: Nearby lies the Coma Galaxy Cluster, over a thousand galaxies some 330 million light-years away. The North Galactic Pole falls in Coma Berenices, making this region extraordinarily rich in background galaxies for telescope observers.

steps.title: Berenice's Hair
steps.caption: Queen Berenice II of Egypt pledged to cut off her famous long hair if her husband returned safely from war. He did, she did, and when the shorn hair vanished from the temple overnight, the court astronomer reported that the gods had placed it among the stars. It's the only modern constellation named after a real person.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Corona Australis — the Southern Crown, a small but delicate arc of stars just south of Sagittarius's Teapot.

steps.title: Below the Teapot
steps.caption: Look just past the base of the Teapot in Sagittarius. Corona Australis is a faint semicircular arc flanking the rich Milky Way below Kaus Australis.

steps.title: A faint semicircle
steps.caption: The crown is fainter than its northern counterpart but the same lovely shape — a curving arc of stars that fits in a single binocular field.

steps.title: A stellar nursery
steps.caption: One of the nearest active stellar nurseries to Earth hides inside the crown, just 420 light-years away. In long-exposure photographs the nebulae glow blue and gold around embedded young stars.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Corona Borealis — the Northern Crown, one of the most charming small constellations: a perfect semicircle of stars resembling a jeweled crown or laurel wreath.

steps.title: Between Arcturus and Vega
steps.caption: Find brilliant orange Arcturus in Boötes — the crown's semicircle sits in the stretch between Arcturus and Vega, making it easy to spot despite its modest stars.

steps.title: Alphecca — the gem in the crown
steps.caption: Alphecca is the brightest point in the arc. Seven stars curve into one of the tightest, most recognizable small patterns in the sky — no similar arc exists nearby.

steps.title: The Blaze Star
steps.caption: The Blaze Star (T CrB) is a recurrent nova lurking near the crown — normally invisible, it erupts to naked-eye brightness roughly every 80 years. It last blew in 1946, and astronomers expect the next eruption any time now.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Corvus — the Crow, a compact quadrilateral that's one of the easiest small constellations to recognize.

steps.title: Arc to Arcturus, speed on to Spica
steps.caption: Follow the Big Dipper's handle to arc to Arcturus, then speed on to Spica. Once you've found Spica, the compact tilted quadrilateral of Corvus is the next obvious group of stars.

steps.title: The compact crow
steps.caption: Four stars of similar brightness form a tight, lopsided quadrilateral — the body of the Crow.

steps.title: The crow and the cup
steps.caption: Corvus and neighboring Crater (the Cup) both sit on the back of Hydra the Water Snake, which stretches across this part of the sky. In myth, Apollo sent the Crow to fetch water in the Cup, but the bird dawdled and was punished — all three were flung into the sky together.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Crater — the Cup, a small faint constellation sitting on the back of Hydra, next to the more prominent Corvus.

steps.title: Next to Corvus on Hydra's back
steps.caption: Find the compact quadrilateral of Corvus, then look for the faint ring of stars next to it, also sitting on Hydra's back. Crater is dim but its circular cup shape is distinctive once you spot it.

steps.title: The Cup
steps.caption: A ring of faint stars traces the outline of a goblet. None are bright, but the shape is satisfying — a nearly complete circle with a short stem.

steps.title: The cup, the crow, and the snake
steps.caption: Apollo sent the Crow to fetch water in this Cup, carried on Hydra the Water Snake — but the bird dawdled, so Apollo flung all three into the sky. That's why Crater, Corvus, and Hydra are still neighbors.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Crux — the Southern Cross, the smallest constellation in the sky and the most iconic in the southern hemisphere.

steps.title: A bright compact kite
steps.caption: Crux is only visible from latitudes south of about 25°N — the further south you are, the higher it rides. Look for a bright, compact kite of four stars embedded in a rich stretch of the Milky Way.

steps.title: The Southern Cross
steps.caption: Four bright stars form the cross: Acrux at the foot is the brightest, Gacrux at the top glows distinctly red, and Mimosa marks one arm.

steps.title: Beware the False Cross
steps.caption: Nearby lies the False Cross — four stars in Vela and Carina that form a similar pattern but are larger and less symmetrical. If your cross looks large and lopsided, you've found the impostor.

steps.title: Finding south
steps.caption: The long axis — from Gacrux through Acrux — points toward the south celestial pole. Extend it about 4.5 times its own length beyond Acrux and you arrive near the pole. Unlike the north, there is no bright star marking it.

steps.title: The Coalsack
steps.caption: Adjacent to Crux lies the Coalsack — a dark nebula, a patch conspicuously empty of stars where interstellar dust blocks the Milky Way behind it. Indigenous Australians saw it as a celestial emu's head, part of a sky mythology drawn in dark shapes rather than star patterns.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Cygnus — the Swan, whose stars form the unmistakable Northern Cross.

steps.title: The Northern Cross in the Milky Way
steps.caption: Cygnus rides through one of the richest stretches of the Milky Way. Look for a large, bright cross shape. It's one of the most recognizable patterns in the sky once you know it.

steps.title: Deneb
steps.caption: Deneb marks the swan's tail at the top of the cross. It looks similar in brightness to nearby stars, but Deneb is a distant supergiant roughly 2,600 light-years away — one of the most intrinsically luminous stars visible to the naked eye.

steps.title: The swan in flight
steps.caption: Sadr sits at the swan's chest in the center of the cross. Albireo at the beak is a celebrated double star, gold and blue in a small telescope.

steps.title: The Summer Triangle and the Great Rift
steps.caption: Deneb forms the Summer Triangle with Vega in Lyra and Altair in Aquila. Cygnus is bisected lengthwise by the Great Rift, a dark lane of interstellar dust that runs through the Milky Way.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Delphinus — the Dolphin, a tiny diamond of stars that genuinely resembles a leaping dolphin.

steps.title: Find Altair, then look nearby
steps.caption: Start with Altair, the bright anchor of Aquila. Delphinus swims just above the eagle's head — a compact diamond shape that's easy to spot once you know where to look.

steps.title: Job's Coffin
steps.caption: The four-star diamond is sometimes called Job's Coffin — nobody knows why. The two brightest stars, Sualocin and Rotanev, are the reversed spelling of Nicolaus Venator — the Latinization of Niccolò Cacciatore, an assistant at Palermo Observatory who snuck his own name into the 1814 star catalog.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Dorado — the Swordfish, a southern constellation best known for containing most of the Large Magellanic Cloud.

steps.title: Start from Canopus
steps.caption: Start from Canopus, the second-brightest star in the sky. The Large Magellanic Cloud — a detached patch of milky light, clearly visible to the naked eye in dark skies — makes Dorado easy to locate.

steps.title: The Large Magellanic Cloud
steps.caption: The LMC is a nearby satellite galaxy of the Milky Way, spanning about seven degrees. It dominates Dorado's territory, dwarfing the constellation's own stars.

steps.title: The Tarantula Nebula
steps.caption: The Tarantula Nebula within the LMC is the largest known emission nebula. The LMC also hosted Supernova 1987A, the nearest supernova observed in nearly 400 years.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Draco — the Dragon, a long winding constellation that coils around the north celestial pole between Ursa Major and Ursa Minor.

steps.title: Between the Bears
steps.caption: Draco winds between Ursa Major and Ursa Minor through the northern sky. The most recognizable feature is the Dragon's Head — a quadrilateral of four stars near Vega.

steps.title: Find the Dragon's Head
steps.caption: The Dragon's Head is a compact trapezoid of four stars with Eltanin as its brightest corner. It's the most distinctive part of this otherwise long, winding constellation.

steps.title: The dragon's long body
steps.caption: From the head, the Dragon's body snakes around Ursa Minor. Thuban, deep in the body, was the pole star around 2700 BC when the pyramids were being built.

steps.title: Heracles and the dragon
steps.caption: In Greek myth, Draco is Ladon, the dragon that guarded the golden apples in the garden of the Hesperides. Heracles killed it as one of his twelve labors. In the sky, Hercules kneels with one foot on the dragon's head. In a Roman telling, Minerva hurled the serpent into the sky during the war with the Titans, where it wrapped around the pole and froze.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Equuleus — the Little Horse, the second-smallest constellation in the sky, a tiny group of faint stars next to Delphinus.

steps.title: Next to Delphinus
steps.caption: Find Delphinus first — the compact diamond of Job's Coffin near Altair. Equuleus sits right next to it, a small pattern that's easy to overlook.

steps.title: The ancient foal
steps.caption: Kitalpha, the brightest star, takes its name from the Arabic for 'part of a horse.' Equuleus is one of Ptolemy's original 48 constellations, recognized since antiquity as a horse's head. Despite being tiny, it has been continuously catalogued for over 2,000 years.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Eridanus — the River, a vast winding constellation stretching from near Orion all the way to the brilliant star Achernar deep in the southern sky.

steps.title: Start near Orion
steps.caption: The river begins at Cursa, a bright star next to Rigel in Orion. From there it meanders a long way across the sky, ending at Achernar, one of the brightest stars in the southern sky.

steps.title: The river's source
steps.caption: Cursa sits next to Rigel and marks where the river pours from Orion's foot. The northern section of Eridanus is a chain of faint stars that wind away from Orion.

steps.title: Achernar — end of the river
steps.caption: Achernar marks the end of the river. It's the ninth-brightest star in the sky, spinning so fast it bulges visibly at its equator.

steps.title: The full river
steps.caption: Eridanus is the sixth-largest constellation, stretching so far across the sky that it's impossible to see in full from any single latitude.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Fornax — the Furnace, a faint southern constellation tucked inside a large southward bend of the river Eridanus.

steps.title: Inside the bend of Eridanus
steps.caption: Eridanus makes two big bends as it snakes from Rigel to Achernar. Fornax sits inside the second bend, with Lepus the Hare looking across the river at it.

steps.title: The Chemical Furnace
steps.caption: Fornax is one of 14 southern constellations invented by Nicolas-Louis de Lacaille in the 1750s. He filled blank patches of the sky with instruments of the Enlightenment. The original name was le Fourneau Chymique — the Chemical Furnace, depicting a distillation furnace with an alembic and receiver.

steps.title: The Fornax Galaxy Cluster
steps.caption: Fornax contains the Fornax Galaxy Cluster, one of the nearest large galaxy clusters. Fornax A and the Great Barred Spiral are its brightest members, both visible in backyard telescopes.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Gemini — the Twins, crowned by the bright pair Castor and Pollux.

steps.title: Near Orion
steps.caption: Gemini lies near Orion in a brilliant star field. From Orion, follow the line from Rigel through Betelgeuse and beyond to find Castor and Pollux, the twin heads.

steps.title: The Twins
steps.caption: Pollux has a warm orange colour that betrays its giant status. Castor, right next to it, is actually six stars — three gravitationally bound pairs — all masquerading as one point of light.

steps.title: The Geminids
steps.caption: Gemini hosts the radiant of the Geminid meteor shower every December, with up to 120 meteors per hour emanating from just above Castor.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Grus — the Crane, a southern constellation with a distinctive kite shape anchored by two bright stars, Alnair and Tiaki.

steps.title: Below Fomalhaut
steps.caption: Start from Fomalhaut, the solitary bright star in Piscis Austrinus. Grus sits below it, with Alnair and Tiaki easy to pick out.

steps.title: Alnair and Tiaki
steps.caption: Alnair is blue-white; Tiaki is a cool red giant. Together they anchor the kite-shaped body of the crane, with a chain of stars extending out as the bird's long neck. The Grus Quartet, a set of four interacting galaxies, is visible in a single telescope field.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Hercules — the Hero, a large constellation identified by the distinctive Keystone asterism and home to M13, a spectacular globular cluster.

steps.title: Between Vega and Arcturus
steps.caption: Draw an imaginary line from Vega to Arcturus — the Keystone of Hercules lies about a third of the way from Vega.

steps.title: Find the Keystone
steps.caption: The Keystone is a quadrilateral of four stars forming the body of the kneeling hero. Once identified, the Keystone is easy to return to.

steps.title: M13 — the Great Globular
steps.caption: The Great Globular Cluster M13 sits on the edge of the Keystone, visible to the naked eye under dark skies as a faint fuzzy star. It contains roughly 300,000 stars packed into a sphere about 145 light-years across.

steps.title: The poisoned cloak
steps.caption: Hercules killed the centaur Nessus for assaulting his wife Deianeira. Dying, Nessus told her to keep his blood as a love charm. Years later she smeared it on a cloak for Hercules, not knowing the blood carried Hydra venom from the arrow that killed Nessus. The poison burned away Hercules' mortal flesh; his immortal half ascended to Olympus, and Zeus placed him in the sky.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Horologium, the Pendulum Clock — a faint constellation tucked between Eridanus and Reticulum.

steps.title: Find it near Achernar
steps.caption: Look for Achernar, the brilliant blue star at the end of Eridanus. Alpha Horologii, an orange giant and the clock's only conspicuous star, sits nearby. The rest are so faint they're a challenge even from dark sites.

steps.title: Lacaille's instruments
steps.caption: During his 1751 survey of the southern sky, Nicolas-Louis de Lacaille filled the gaps between ancient constellations with 14 new ones, all named after instruments of the Enlightenment. Horologium honors Huygens's pendulum clock, which made accurate navigation at sea possible for the first time.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Hydra, the Water Snake — the largest constellation in the sky, winding across more than a hundred degrees.

steps.title: The solitary one
steps.caption: Look for Alphard, a lonely orange star glowing in an otherwise empty stretch of sky. Its Arabic name means 'the solitary one.' Follow a line from Castor through Pollux to find it.

steps.title: The longest snake
steps.caption: A ring of faint stars marks the snake's head, and the body winds on and on with the Crow and the Cup riding on its back. Apollo sent the crow to fetch water in the cup, but it dawdled eating figs and brought back a water snake as an excuse. Apollo flung all three into the sky.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Hydrus, the Lesser Water Snake — a small triangle of stars near the Magellanic Clouds, not to be confused with the much larger Hydra.

steps.title: Between the Clouds
steps.caption: In the area dominated by Achernar and the two Magellanic Clouds you'll find a triangle of three stars. Beta Hydri, the one near the Small Cloud, is the nearest bright star to the south celestial pole.

steps.title: Our Sun's future
steps.caption: At just 24 light-years away, Beta Hydri is one of the nearest Sun-like stars. It's old enough to have evolved slightly off the main sequence — a preview of what our own Sun will look like in about a billion years.

steps.title: Charting new stars
steps.caption: In 1595, Dutch navigator Pieter Keyser began charting stars invisible from Europe during a trade voyage to the East Indies. He died at sea, but his colleague Frederick de Houtman completed the catalog — adding twelve new constellations to the southern sky, Hydrus among them.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Indus, the Indian — a faint constellation between Pavo and Grus.

steps.title: Find it near Alnair
steps.caption: Look for Alnair in Grus, then find Alpha Indi nearby — an orange giant and the constellation's only conspicuous star.

steps.title: A nearby neighbor
steps.caption: Epsilon Indi is one of the nearest Sun-like stars at just 12 light-years. It hosts a pair of brown dwarfs — objects too small to sustain hydrogen fusion but larger than any planet.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Lacerta, the Lizard — a small constellation sandwiched between Cygnus and Cassiopeia.

steps.title: A faint zigzag
steps.caption: Look for a meandering chain of faint stars in the gap between Deneb and the W of Cassiopeia, embedded in a rich stretch of the Milky Way.

steps.title: BL Lacertae
steps.caption: Lacerta gives its name to BL Lacertae objects — a class of active galactic nuclei whose jets point nearly straight at Earth. BL Lac itself was first cataloged as a variable star before its extragalactic nature was discovered.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Leo, the Lion — one of the sky's most recognizable constellations, anchored by bright Regulus.

steps.title: The Pointer Stars
steps.caption: The Big Dipper's Pointer Stars — Dubhe and Merak — point toward Polaris in one direction and Regulus in the other.

steps.title: The Sickle and the Lion
steps.caption: Leo's head and mane form a backward question mark called the Sickle. Regulus, a hot blue-white star, anchors the base. A triangle of stars traces the haunches and tail, with Denebola at the tip.

steps.title: The Nemean Lion
steps.caption: Leo represents the Nemean Lion, a beast whose hide no weapon could pierce. Heracles tracked it to its cave, blocked one entrance, and strangled it with his bare hands. He wore the lion's pelt as armor for the rest of his labors.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Leo Minor, the Lesser Lion — a faint constellation wedged between Leo and the Big Dipper.

steps.title: Between Leo and the Dipper
steps.caption: Leo Minor lies between Regulus and the Big Dipper's bowl. Praecipua is the only easily visible star in the gap.

steps.title: Filling the gap
steps.caption: Polish astronomer Johannes Hevelius created Leo Minor in 1687 from unclaimed stars between Leo and Ursa Major. It has no mythology — just a small lion keeping the big one company.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Lepus — the Hare, a small constellation crouching directly beneath Orion.

steps.title: Below Orion's feet
steps.caption: Lepus sits just below Orion's feet — Saiph and Rigel point the way. Look for a compact group of stars immediately below the hunter.

steps.title: Arneb and Nihal
steps.caption: Arneb and Nihal are the two brightest stars in Lepus, forming the Hare's neck. The Hare was placed at Orion's feet as his quarry, forever fleeing the hunter across the sky.

steps.title: Hind's Crimson Star
steps.caption: Hind's Crimson Star, near the edge of Lepus, is one of the deepest red stars visible to the naked eye — a pulsating carbon star nearing the end of its life.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Libra — the Scales, the only zodiacal constellation representing an inanimate object, sandwiched between Virgo and Scorpius along the ecliptic.

steps.title: Between Spica and Antares
steps.caption: Libra sits between Spica in Virgo and Antares in Scorpius. Its two brightest stars, Zubenelgenubi and Zubeneschamali, form an obvious pair between these landmarks.

steps.title: The scorpion's old claws
steps.caption: Zubeneschamali is one of the few stars consistently described as greenish by observers. Zubenelgenubi is a wide naked-eye double, easily split with binoculars into a white pair. Their Arabic names mean 'northern claw' and 'southern claw,' recalling a time when they were considered part of Scorpius.

steps.title: The scales of the zodiac
steps.caption: The Methuselah Star, inside Libra's boundary, is one of the oldest known stars — nearly as old as the universe itself at around 13.7 billion years. It's faint but visible in binoculars, a relic from a time before the Milky Way had fully formed.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Lupus — the Wolf, a southern constellation nestled between Centaurus and Scorpius along the Milky Way.

steps.title: Between Centaurus and Scorpius
steps.caption: Lupus sits between Centaurus and Scorpius along the Milky Way. Look up from the Southern Pointers, Rigil Kentaurus and Hadar, to find Alpha Lupi, the Wolf's brightest star.

steps.title: The Wolf
steps.caption: Lupus has a generous scattering of bright stars but no single memorable shape. The Greeks saw it as a wild animal impaled on a spear held by Centaurus — the two constellations were often depicted together.

steps.title: Supernova 1006
steps.caption: In 1006 AD, a supernova blazed in Lupus — the brightest stellar event in recorded history. Egyptian astrologer Ali ibn Ridwan described it as a quarter the brightness of the Moon; Chinese astronomers tracked the 'guest star' for over a year. Its expanding remnant is now a faint X-ray and radio shell.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Lynx — a long, faint northern constellation so named by Polish astronomer Johannes Hevelius because only someone with the eyes of a lynx could see it, filling the gap between Auriga and Ursa Major.

steps.title: Between Capella and the Dipper
steps.caption: Lynx fills a large, empty gap between bright Capella and the Big Dipper's bowl. Scan the relatively barren stretch between them.

steps.title: Alpha Lyncis
steps.caption: Alpha Lyncis is the sole beacon of the constellation — an orange-red giant and the only star here brighter than fourth magnitude. The long chain of fainter stars requires dark skies to trace.

steps.title: The Intergalactic Wanderer
steps.caption: NGC 2419, the Intergalactic Wanderer, is a globular cluster about 300,000 light-years away — farther than the Magellanic Clouds. It orbits so far from the galactic center that astronomers once doubted it was bound to the Milky Way at all, earning it the name.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Lyra — a small constellation home to Vega, the fifth-brightest star in the sky.

steps.title: The Summer Triangle
steps.caption: Vega is the brightest corner of the Summer Triangle, an asterism it shares with Deneb in Cygnus and Altair in Aquila. Vega is the brightest of the three — impossible to miss.

steps.title: Vega
steps.caption: Vega was the original magnitude standard — astronomers defined it as exactly zero, the reference point for all stellar brightness. It was the north pole star around 12,000 BCE and will be again around 13,700 CE.

steps.title: The Lyre
steps.caption: Just below Vega, four fainter stars form a small parallelogram — the frame of the lyre. The Ring Nebula, easily found in a small telescope, lies between the two lower stars of the parallelogram.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Mensa — the Table Mountain, notable mainly because the southern edge of the Large Magellanic Cloud overlaps it.

steps.title: Under the LMC
steps.caption: Mensa sits just below the Large Magellanic Cloud — the LMC's edge actually spills across the boundary into Mensa. None of Mensa's stars are bright, so the Cloud is the easiest way to find it.

steps.title: Table Mountain
steps.caption: French astronomer Nicolas-Louis de Lacaille named this constellation after Table Mountain near Cape Town — the flat-topped landmark overlooking his observing site during his 1751–52 survey of the southern sky. It is the faintest of all 88 constellations.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Microscopium — the Microscope, a faint constellation tucked below Capricornus.

steps.title: Below Capricornus
steps.caption: Microscopium sits below Capricornus, not far from Fomalhaut, in an empty stretch of sky with no bright stars. Follow the point of Capricornus's arrowhead to find its faint pattern.

steps.title: Lacaille's microscope
steps.caption: During his 1751 survey of the southern sky, Nicolas-Louis de Lacaille filled the gaps between ancient constellations with 14 new ones, all named after instruments of the Enlightenment. Microscopium honors the compound microscope — the revolutionary instrument that opened the invisible world to science.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Monoceros — the Unicorn, a faint constellation filling the space between Orion, Canis Major, and Canis Minor.

steps.title: Inside the Winter Triangle
steps.caption: Monoceros fills the interior of the Winter Triangle formed by Sirius, Procyon, and Betelgeuse — three of the brightest stars in the sky. The Unicorn's own stars are faint, but the landmarks around it are unmistakable.

steps.title: Beta Monocerotis
steps.caption: Monoceros was invented around 1612 by Dutch cartographer Petrus Plancius to fill the gap between Orion's dogs. The unicorn may come from the biblical 're'em' — really a wild ox, but mistranslated as 'unicorn' in the King James Bible. Beta Monocerotis, its finest treasure, is a triple star system resolvable in any small telescope.

steps.title: The Rosette Nebula
steps.caption: The Rosette Nebula, one of the sky's most photogenic objects, appears as a full-moon-sized glowing ring about 5,200 light-years away. The star cluster at its center blew a cavity in the surrounding gas and is visible in binoculars.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Musca — the Fly, a small, compact southern constellation just past the Southern Cross.

steps.title: Past the Southern Cross
steps.caption: Find the Southern Cross and look just beyond Acrux, at the foot of the Cross. Musca's compact diamond of stars sits right there.

steps.title: A compact diamond
steps.caption: Dutch explorers Keyser and de Houtman mapped Musca in the 1590s and named it the Fly, but their illustration on Plancius's globe was unlabeled. When Bayer copied the unnamed insect into his influential 1603 star atlas, he guessed wrong and called it Apis — the Bee. The mistake stuck for two centuries until Lacaille corrected it.

steps.title: The Dark Doodad
steps.caption: Musca is home to the Dark Doodad — a narrow lane of interstellar dust named for its squiggly shape. About 700 light-years away and 30 light-years long, this molecular cloud blocks the light of countless stars behind it.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Norma — the Carpenter's Square, a small faint constellation embedded in the rich southern Milky Way between Scorpius and Centaurus.

steps.title: Between Scorpius and Centaurus
steps.caption: Norma sits between the scorpion's tail and the centaur's foot. Scan from the Southern Pointers toward Shaula on the scorpion's stinger. Norma is a faint group in between, embedded in a spectacular stretch of Milky Way.

steps.title: Lacaille's square
steps.caption: French astronomer Nicolas-Louis de Lacaille named this constellation l'Équerre et la Règle — a draughtsman's set square and rule. Its two brightest stars, representing the rule, were transferred to Scorpius when constellation boundaries were redrawn in 1930, leaving only the faint shape of the square.

steps.title: The Great Attractor
steps.caption: This brilliant star cloud hides one of the great mysteries in cosmology. Somewhere behind all this gas and dust lies the Great Attractor — a concentration of mass equivalent to a million billion suns, pulling the Milky Way and everything around it toward this spot at 600 km/s. We can barely see it because it's in the Zone of Avoidance, the band of sky obscured by our own galaxy's disk. The Norma Galaxy Cluster, near its center, was only confirmed through X-ray and radio observations that can peer through the dust.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Octans — the Octant, the constellation that contains the south celestial pole. Unlike the north, where Polaris serves as a convenient landmark, the south has no bright star to mark the spot.

steps.title: Finding the south celestial pole
steps.caption: The practical way to find the south celestial pole is through the Southern Cross. Extend the long axis of Crux past Acrux and it points almost straight to the pole. You can also draw a line perpendicular to the Southern Pointers — the two lines intersect near the pole.

steps.title: Waiting for a pole star
steps.caption: Sigma Octantis, the nearest naked-eye star to the pole, is so dim it's invisible from any light-polluted site — a poor substitute for Polaris. The south pole's lack of a bright marker is a temporary cosmic accident. Precession is slowly shifting Earth's axis, and around 9250 AD, Delta Velorum will come within 0.2° of the south pole — closer than Polaris ever gets to the north pole.

steps.title: Lacaille's octant
steps.caption: During his 1751 survey of the southern sky, Nicolas-Louis de Lacaille filled the gaps between ancient constellations with 14 new ones, all named after instruments of the Enlightenment. Octans honors the reflecting octant, a navigation instrument that preceded the sextant.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Ophiuchus — the Serpent Bearer, a large constellation straddling the celestial equator between Scorpius and Hercules.

steps.title: Above Antares
steps.caption: Start at brilliant Antares in Scorpius and scan above it, with the Milky Way on your left. Five stars there define the central pentagon of the serpent bearer's body. The topmost and brightest, Rasalhague, locates the head.

steps.title: Bearer and serpent
steps.caption: Ophiuchus represents Asclepius, the Greek god of medicine, holding a serpent — the origin of the Rod of Asclepius still used as a medical symbol. What you see here is actually two constellations drawn as one figure: the serpent is Serpens, the only constellation split into two disconnected pieces — its head and tail separated by the body of Ophiuchus.

steps.title: Kepler's Star
steps.caption: In October 1604, a new star appeared in Ophiuchus, outshining Jupiter and remaining visible in daylight for three weeks. Kepler, Galileo, and astronomers in Korea and China all recorded it. It was the last supernova observed in our galaxy — and over four centuries later, no one has seen another.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Orion — arguably the most recognizable constellation in the sky, visible from every inhabited place on Earth.

steps.title: The Belt
steps.caption: Orion straddles the celestial equator, so it's visible from almost everywhere on Earth. Start with the Belt — three bright stars of nearly equal brightness in a perfectly straight row. Nothing else in the sky looks quite like it.

steps.title: Three in a row
steps.caption: Alnitak, Alnilam, and Mintaka — the Belt stars are a tight, unmistakable line that stands out from far across the sky. Once you've found them, the rest of the Hunter falls into place around them.

steps.title: The Hunter
steps.caption: At one shoulder glows Betelgeuse — a red supergiant nearing the end of its life. At the opposite foot blazes Rigel — brilliant blue-white and one of the most luminous stars in the galaxy. The color contrast between them is visible to the naked eye.

steps.title: The sky's signpost
steps.caption: The Belt is one of the most useful landmarks in the sky. Follow it one way and you reach Aldebaran, the red eye of Taurus, and the Pleiades beyond it. Follow it the other way and you reach Sirius — the brightest star in the entire night sky.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Pavo — the Peacock, one of the "Southern Birds" created by Dutch navigators Keyser and de Houtman during their 1595 voyage to the East Indies.

steps.title: Near the Crane
steps.caption: Look for a bright, isolated blue-white star not far from Alnair in Grus. That's Peacock, the constellation's brightest star — a name it only got in the 1930s, when the Royal Air Force insisted that all navigation stars must have proper names.

steps.title: Hera's sacred bird
steps.caption: In Greek myth, the peacock was sacred to Hera. When she set the hundred-eyed giant Argus to guard Zeus's lover Io, Hermes killed him — and Hera placed Argus's eyes on her peacock's tail. The constellation itself probably depicts the green peafowl that Keyser and de Houtman encountered in Java, not the blue peacock the Greeks knew.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Pegasus — the Winged Horse, whose four bright stars form the Great Square of Pegasus, one of the most recognizable asterisms in the sky.

steps.title: The Great Square
steps.caption: Look for a large, empty square of four stars. The interior is strikingly bare — this emptiness is what makes it so easy to spot. One corner, Alpheratz, is shared with Andromeda.

steps.title: The myth of Andromeda
steps.caption: Pegasus sprang from the blood of Medusa when Perseus beheaded her. It's part of the largest mythological group in the sky — six neighboring constellations tell the story of Andromeda's rescue from a sea monster.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Perseus — the Hero, lying in the Milky Way between Cassiopeia and Taurus.

steps.title: Below Cassiopeia
steps.caption: Look below Cassiopeia's W shape for a bright star embedded in the Milky Way. That's Mirfak, Perseus's brightest star, surrounded by a loose cluster of young blue-white stars visible in binoculars.

steps.title: The myth of Andromeda
steps.caption: Perseus is the hero who slew the Gorgon Medusa and rescued Princess Andromeda from the sea monster Cetus — riding in on the winged horse Pegasus, born from Medusa's blood. Six constellations in this part of the sky represent figures in the story.

steps.title: The Demon Star
steps.caption: Algol is the "Demon Star" — it dims noticeably every 2.87 days when a fainter companion star passes in front of it. Ancient Arabic astronomers named it Ra's al-Ghul, the demon's head, and it marks the severed head of Medusa in Perseus's hand. It was one of the first variable stars recognized by modern astronomy.

steps.title: The Double Cluster
steps.caption: Between Perseus and Cassiopeia lies one of the finest sights in the sky — the Double Cluster, two rich star clusters side by side, visible as a fuzzy patch to the naked eye and spectacular through binoculars. Each cluster contains hundreds of young blue and red supergiants.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Phoenix is one of a flock of southern birds mapped by Keyser and de Houtman in the 1590s, alongside Grus, Pavo, and Tucana. Look for it in the sparse stretch of sky between Fomalhaut and Achernar.

steps.title: Between two bright stars
steps.caption: Ankaa is the brightest star in the gap between Fomalhaut and Achernar. Its name comes from the Arabic al-ʽanqāʼ, meaning 'the phoenix.' It marks the head of the bird.

steps.title: Rising from the ashes
steps.caption: The phoenix descends from the Egyptian Bennu, a solar bird linked to Ra. Herodotus tells the story: every 500 years the bird builds a nest of cinnamon and myrrh at the Temple of the Sun in Heliopolis, sets it alight, and is reborn from the ashes.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Pictor — the Painter's Easel, which lies between brilliant Canopus and Achernar in the deep southern sky. Its brightest star, Alpha Pictoris (mag 3.2), is barely conspicuous without context.

steps.title: Find Pictor's region
steps.caption: Look roughly 30° east of Canopus along the southern sky. Alpha Pictoris is the faint star in that region. Beta Pictoris (mag 3.9) is famous for its debris disk and imaged planets.

steps.title: Pictor's stars
steps.caption: The constellation is a simple line of faint stars. Alpha Pictoris anchors the south end; Beta Pictoris (the famous planet-forming star) is to the northwest.

steps.title: Pictor's full extent
steps.caption: Pictor is best seen December–February from the southern hemisphere. Northern observers above 26°N cannot see it.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Pisces — the Fish, a faint zodiacal constellation south of Andromeda with no star brighter than magnitude 3.7, representing two fish tied together by their tails.

steps.title: Look south of Andromeda in autumn
steps.caption: In October and November, Pisces is high in the sky for northern observers. The Great Square of Pegasus is the key landmark — Pisces lies south and east of the Square. The Circlet of Pisces, a ring of five faint stars (the western fish's head), sits about 15° south-southwest of the Square's southeastern corner, Markab.

steps.title: Find the Circlet
steps.caption: The Circlet of Pisces is a ring of five stars (magnitudes 4–5) about 4° across, forming the most identifiable sub-pattern in the constellation. It represents the head of the western fish. Under suburban skies the Circlet can be difficult; look for it as a faint loop of stars south of the Great Square.

steps.title: Two fish and the knot
steps.caption: Alrescha is the 'Knot' where the two fish are tied together, located in the eastern part of the constellation. Pisces currently hosts the vernal equinox due to precession from its historical location in Aries.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Piscis Austrinus — the Southern Fish, whose one brilliant star Fomalhaut stands completely alone in the autumn sky, earning it the name 'the Lonely Star of Autumn.'

steps.title: Look south in autumn
steps.caption: In September and October evenings, Fomalhaut is the brightest star low in the southern sky for northern observers — there are no other first-magnitude stars anywhere near it. From the Great Square of Pegasus, extend its western side southward about 45° and you land almost exactly on Fomalhaut. From the southern hemisphere it is well-placed and high in the sky.

steps.title: Find Fomalhaut
steps.caption: Fomalhaut (Alpha PsA, mag 1.2) is a blue-white A-type star about 25 light-years away — one of the Sun's relatively close neighbors. It is famous for hosting one of the first directly imaged debris disks, and a candidate exoplanet (Fomalhaut b) was detected orbiting within this disk. The star's isolation in the sky is striking: the nearest comparably bright star is hundreds of degrees away.

steps.title: The southern fish
steps.caption: The rest of Piscis Austrinus consists of a curved arc of faint stars (magnitudes 4–5) arranged in a fish shape around Fomalhaut. The full constellation fits within about 20° and is not particularly prominent beyond its one bright star. Fomalhaut's isolation makes it useful as an autumn benchmark: when you can see it, the southern sky is reasonably transparent.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Puppis — the Ship's Stern, the largest fragment of the ancient constellation Argo Navis, sitting in the winter Milky Way east of Sirius.

steps.title: Look east of Sirius in winter
steps.caption: In January through March, Puppis is in the southern sky east of the brilliant Sirius. From Sirius, move east and slightly south to enter Puppis's star fields. For northern observers above 30°N only the northern part of the constellation is visible; from the southern hemisphere the full constellation is well-placed.

steps.title: Find Naos and the bright stars
steps.caption: Naos (Zeta Puppis, mag 2.2) is one of the most luminous and hottest naked-eye stars — a blue O-type supergiant with a surface temperature around 42,000 K, about 1,100 light-years away. It is also a powerful source of ultraviolet radiation. The magnitude 1.8 star at the constellation's southwestern corner (Pi Puppis) provides a second bright anchor near Canopus in Carina.

steps.title: Star clusters in the stern
steps.caption: Puppis is in the Milky Way and contains several showpiece open clusters: M46 and M47 are 1.5° apart in the northern part of the constellation — M47 (mag 4.4) is visible naked-eye and M46 (mag 6.1) is a rich binocular cluster with a planetary nebula inside. M93 is another nearby cluster. The sheer density of background stars makes Puppis a rewarding sweep with any optics.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Pyxis — the Compass, a a small constellation in the Milky Way, carved from the ancient ship Argo. It sits just north of Vela and east of Puppis.

steps.title: Find Pyxis's region
steps.caption: Find Pyxis about 15° north of Gamma Velorum, or roughly halfway between Sirius and Gamma Velorum in the southern Milky Way. Naos in Puppis (mag 2.2) is a bright blue reference to the west.

steps.title: Pyxis's stars
steps.caption: Alpha Pyxidis (mag 3.7, blue) is the brightest star. Beta Pyxidis is close by to the southwest. The three main stars form a short north-south line.

steps.title: Pyxis's extent
steps.caption: Pyxis is best seen January–March. It is sometimes considered the compass of the mythological ship Argo.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Reticulum — the Reticle, a a small southern constellation near the Large Magellanic Cloud. Its most recognizable feature is a small rhombus (diamond) of four stars.

steps.title: Find Reticulum
steps.caption: Locate the LMC and look about 20° southeast of it. Alpha Reticuli (mag 3.4) and Beta Reticuli (mag 3.8) are the two brightest stars of the small diamond shape.

steps.title: The rhombus shape
steps.caption: The four stars form a compact rhombus — one of the more recognizable patterns among the southern minor constellations. Alpha is brightest at the east vertex.

steps.title: Reticulum's extent
steps.caption: Reticulum is best seen October–January from the southern hemisphere. Northern observers above 23°N cannot see it.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Sagitta — the Arrow, which is tiny but unmistakable — it genuinely looks like an arrow in the summer Milky Way between Cygnus and Aquila.

steps.title: Find Sagitta
steps.caption: Find Sagitta between Albireo (the colorful head star of Cygnus) and Altair in Aquila. The arrow points eastward.

steps.title: The arrow stars
steps.caption: Delta Sagittae is the brightest star in the arrow. Sagitta is one of the smallest constellations. Globular cluster M71 lies along the arrow shaft — an easy binocular target. Best seen August–September.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Sagittarius — a southern constellation best known for its distinctive Teapot asterism, pointing toward the heart of the Milky Way.

steps.title: Face south in summer
steps.caption: Turn to the southern sky on a summer night. The Milky Way arcs through this region — the bright, hazy band is our galaxy seen edge-on. Sagittarius sits right along it, near the galactic core.

steps.title: Find Scorpius first
steps.caption: Start with Scorpius — its distinctive fishhook curve is easy to recognise. Antares, the bright orange-red star at its heart, is one of the biggest stars in the sky. From Antares the tail curves down and east to a stinging pair of stars.

steps.title: Look east of Scorpius
steps.caption: Now shift your gaze to the left (east) of Scorpius's tail. Sagittarius sits right beside it — the spout of its Teapot asterism aims directly back toward Scorpius. If you can find the scorpion, the teapot is right next door.

steps.title: The Teapot asterism
steps.caption: Here's the Teapot — eight stars of 2nd and 3rd magnitude forming the outline of a teapot. The spout (three stars, left) points west toward Scorpius. The handle (two stars, right) points east. A triangular lid sits on top.

steps.title: Key stars
steps.caption: Kaus Australis (ε Sgr, mag 1.8) is the brightest star in Sagittarius — it sits at the bottom of the spout. Nunki (σ Sgr, mag 2.0) marks the top of the handle at upper right. Memorise these two and the rest follows.

steps.title: Steam from the spout
steps.caption: Pull back and look at the big picture. The 'steam' rising from the Teapot's spout is the brightest part of the Milky Way — the galactic core. Now you can find it without any guides.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Scorpius — one of the most magnificent constellations, shaped unmistakably like a scorpion curling through the summer Milky Way.

steps.title: Look south in summer
steps.caption: Look low to the southern sky on a summer evening. In July, Scorpius hugs the southern horizon for northern observers. In the southern hemisphere it rides high overhead, fully revealing its spectacular curved tail.

steps.title: Find Antares
steps.caption: The easiest entry point is Antares — a brilliant red-orange supergiant marking the scorpion's heart. It is one of the largest stars visible to the naked eye, and its distinctly reddish colour is obvious at a glance.

steps.title: The scorpion's curve
steps.caption: Stars arc north of Antares to form the head and claws, while the tail sweeps south and east, curling back on itself through the Milky Way. Shaula marks the tip of the sting — best seen from latitudes below about 35°N where the tail clears the horizon.

steps.title: Hunter and scorpion
steps.caption: Scorpius rides the Milky Way with the galactic core glowing just to its east in Sagittarius. Ancient myth says Scorpius and Orion the Hunter chase each other across the sky — when one rises in the east, the other sets in the west.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Sculptor — it's a large but very faint constellation south of Aquarius and Piscis Austrinus. No star is brighter than mag 4.3, but it harbors the spectacular NGC 253 galaxy.

steps.title: Find Sculptor's region
steps.caption: Find the area about 25° south of Fomalhaut (the bright Lonely Star of Autumn in Piscis Austrinus). The faint stars of Sculptor are in that sparse southern region.

steps.title: Sculptor's stars
steps.caption: Alpha Sculptoris (mag 4.3, blue) is the brightest. Gamma and Delta are spread to the west. The constellation is faint but its area contains the south galactic pole.

steps.title: Sculptor's extent
steps.caption: Despite its faintness, Sculptor holds NGC 253 (Sculptor Galaxy), one of the nearest and brightest spirals — spectacular in binoculars. Best seen October–November.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Scutum — the Shield, which sits in one of the richest parts of the Milky Way, just south of Aquila. The Scutum Star Cloud makes this region visibly bright to the naked eye.

steps.title: Find Scutum
steps.caption: Find Scutum about 10° south of Altair in Aquila. Look for the noticeably bright patch of the Milky Way — the Scutum Star Cloud — in that region.

steps.title: Scutum's stars
steps.caption: Beta Scuti (mag 3.9, orange) and Alpha (mag 4.2) are the main stars, but the star cloud background is more impressive than any individual star here.

steps.title: Scutum's extent
steps.caption: The Wild Duck Cluster (M11) is one of the richest open clusters in the sky — a dense, spectacular swarm. Best seen July–August from both hemispheres.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Serpens — the Serpent, the only constellation split into two separate parts in the sky, divided by the large figure of Ophiuchus the Serpent Bearer.

steps.title: Look in the summer sky
steps.caption: In June and July, Serpens is well-placed for northern observers. Serpens Caput (the head) lies west of Ophiuchus, visible as a small bent chain of stars below Corona Borealis. Serpens Cauda (the tail) is on the opposite side of Ophiuchus, in the summer Milky Way east of Rasalhague.

steps.title: Find Unukalhai in the head
steps.caption: Unukalhai (Alpha Serpentis, mag 2.6) is the constellation's brightest star and belongs to Serpens Caput. It is an orange giant about 74 light-years away. From Arcturus in Boötes, move about 30° to the south-southeast to find Unukalhai. A small bent chain of stars — the serpent's head and neck — extends east from it toward Ophiuchus.

steps.title: Caput and Cauda
steps.caption: Serpens Cauda contains the Eagle Nebula (M16), a famous star-forming region 7,000 light-years away famous from Hubble's 'Pillars of Creation' image — visible in binoculars as a fuzzy patch. The full serpent, connecting the head and tail across Ophiuchus, is one of the most unusual and mythologically rich constellations in the sky.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Sextans — the Sextant, a a faint equatorial constellation south of Leo. No star is brighter than mag 4.5, so use nearby bright Regulus as your anchor.

steps.title: Find Sextans
steps.caption: Move about 15° due south of Regulus (the bright heart of Leo). Sextans occupies the sparse area between Leo and Hydra along the celestial equator.

steps.title: Sextans's stars
steps.caption: The stars of Sextans are all faint (mag 4.5 or dimmer). Beta Sextantis anchors the northern edge. The constellation is best seen by its position between Regulus and Hydra.

steps.title: Sextans's extent
steps.caption: Sextans is a modern constellation (Hevelius, 1687) with no mythology and no memorable pattern. Best seen March–April for northern observers.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Taurus — the Bull, a winter constellation anchored by brilliant orange-red Aldebaran and the iconic Pleiades star cluster.

steps.title: The winter sky
steps.caption: Turn to the southern sky on a winter evening. Taurus lies northwest of Orion in the same brilliant winter star field, prominent from November through February and visible from both hemispheres.

steps.title: Follow the Belt
steps.caption: If you can find Orion's Belt, Aldebaran is easy: follow the three belt stars northwest about 20° and you run straight into it. Aldebaran's distinctly orange-red colour makes it unmistakable — it is the angry eye of the charging bull.

steps.title: The V-face and the Pleiades
steps.caption: Aldebaran sits at the tip of the V-shaped Hyades cluster — the bull's face. Although it looks like part of the V, Aldebaran is a foreground star at just 65 light-years, while the Hyades lie 150 light-years away. The Pleiades jewel-box glitters to the northwest, marking the bull's shoulder.

steps.title: The Bull
steps.caption: Taurus rewards slow looking: the Hyades V, orange Aldebaran, the Pleiades jewel-box, and the long horns extending northeast. On a clear night, count how many Pleiades you can see unaided — most people spot six or seven, though sharp eyes find more.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Telescopium — the Telescope, a a small, faint southern constellation between Corona Australis and Pavo. Alpha Telescopii (mag 3.5, blue) is its sole reasonable landmark.

steps.title: Find Telescopium
steps.caption: Find it about 20° south of Corona Australis (which sits below Sagittarius), or about 15° north of Peacock to the south.

steps.title: Telescopium's stars
steps.caption: Alpha Telescopii (mag 3.5, blue) and Zeta (mag 4.5) are the two main stars. The constellation forms a thin triangular arc of faint stars.

steps.title: Telescopium's extent
steps.caption: Telescopium is best seen July–August from the southern hemisphere. Northern observers above 33°N cannot see it.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Triangulum — the Triangle, a a small northern constellation forming an obvious narrow elongated triangle between Andromeda and Aries.

steps.title: Find Triangulum
steps.caption: Find Triangulum about 20° south of Andromeda's main chain, or 12° northwest of the Pleiades. Beta Trianguli (mag 3.0) is the brightest of the three triangle stars.

steps.title: The triangle stars
steps.caption: Three stars form the narrow triangle: Beta (mag 3.0, west), Gamma (mag 4.0, east tip), and Alpha Mothallah (mag 3.4, south). The elongated shape points east-northeast.

steps.title: Triangulum's extent
steps.caption: M33 (the Triangulum Galaxy) is the third-largest Local Group member — a large faint glow under excellent dark skies. Best seen October–December.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Triangulum Australe — the Southern Triangle, which is one of the most easily recognized far-southern constellations — a compact, bright equilateral triangle with Atria (mag 1.9) at the south vertex.

steps.title: Find the Southern Triangle
steps.caption: Find it about 25° east of the Southern Pointers (Alpha and Beta Centauri). Atria at mag 1.9 is the brightest and southernmost vertex of the compact triangle.

steps.title: The southern triangle
steps.caption: Three bright stars form a compact equilateral triangle: Atria (Alpha, mag 1.9, red, south), Beta (mag 2.8, northwest), and Delta (mag 2.9, northeast).

steps.title: Triangulum Australe's extent
steps.caption: The Southern Triangle is entirely invisible north of about 25°N. Best seen April–June from the southern hemisphere.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Tucana — the Toucan, which is best known for hosting the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) and the brilliant globular cluster 47 Tucanae, which appears as a fuzzy star right beside the SMC.

steps.title: Find Tucana
steps.caption: Locate the SMC — a detached glowing cloud visible to the naked eye in the far south, about 20° east of Achernar in Eridanus. Alpha Tucanae (mag 2.9) anchors the stellar part of the constellation.

steps.title: Alpha Tucanae and 47 Tuc
steps.caption: Alpha Tucanae (mag 2.9, orange-red) is the main stellar anchor. 47 Tucanae (NGC 104) appears to the naked eye as a fuzzy star nestled against the edge of the SMC — a magnificent globular.

steps.title: Tucana's extent
steps.caption: Tucana is best seen September–November from southern latitudes. The SMC sits within the constellation boundaries — one of the sky's finest showpieces.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Ursa Major — home of the Big Dipper, one of the most recognisable star patterns in the northern sky.

steps.title: Look north
steps.caption: Turn to the northern sky. Ursa Major is circumpolar for most northern hemisphere observers — it never sets, wheeling around the pole through the night and seasons. In spring it rides highest overhead.

steps.title: The Big Dipper
steps.caption: The Big Dipper is unmistakable: four stars form the bowl, three arc away to form the handle. The two stars on the outer edge of the bowl — Dubhe and Merak — are the Pointer Stars, your most useful navigational tool in the northern sky.

steps.title: Follow the Pointers
steps.caption: Draw a line from Merak through Dubhe and keep going about 29° — five times the gap between them — and you land on Polaris, the North Star. Polaris marks the celestial north pole and barely moves all night while every other star circles around it.

steps.title: The Great Bear
steps.caption: The Big Dipper is just the back and tail of Ursa Major — the Great Bear. Fainter stars extend outward to trace the head, shoulders and paws. It is the third-largest constellation in the sky and one of the oldest recognised star figures in human history.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: Let's find Ursa Minor — the Little Bear, whose tail-tip star Polaris marks the north celestial pole and has guided navigators for centuries.

steps.title: Look north, any night of the year
steps.caption: Face north from anywhere in the northern hemisphere and you can find Polaris, which sits within 1° of the true celestial pole and barely moves throughout the night. The Little Dipper is circumpolar and always above the horizon at any northern latitude, though its faint stars require reasonably dark skies.

steps.title: Use the Big Dipper as a pointer
steps.caption: The two 'Pointer Stars' at the outer edge of the Big Dipper's bowl — Merak (mag 2.4) and Dubhe (mag 1.8) — point directly to Polaris. Extend the line from Merak through Dubhe about five times the distance between them and you land on Polaris (mag 2.0). Polaris is not the brightest star, so many beginners are surprised by how modest it looks.

steps.title: The Little Dipper
steps.caption: Polaris marks the tip of the Little Dipper's handle. The bowl opens toward Kochab (mag 2.1) and Pherkad (mag 3.0), the two brightest bowl stars and the most visible parts of the Little Bear in suburban skies. The handle curves from Polaris back toward a dim arc of stars.

steps.title: The ancient pole star
steps.caption: Due to Earth's precession, the celestial pole slowly traces a circle among the stars over 26,000 years. Around 2700 BC, the star Thuban in Draco was the pole star; Polaris will reach its closest approach to the true pole around 2100 AD before slowly drifting away.
